Description: Establishes a carpet collection program. Beginning December 31, 2024, prohibits the sale of carpets containing perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.

Toxic Chemical: Heavy Metals, Phthalates, Toxic Flame Retardants, Formaldehyde, Mercury, PFAS, Lead, Bisphenols, Toxic Chemicals General
Description: Requires manufacturers of children’s products containing dangerous chemicals to notify the state and retailers; bans the sale of children’s products containing dangerous chemicals.
Prohibits intentionally added PFAS in consumer products including carpets, cookware, cosmetics, fabric treatments, and personal care items.
Establishes maximum contaminant levels and a hazard index in drinking water for certain PFAS.
Requires a manufacturer that produced, sold, or distributed a class B firefighting foam that contains intentionally added PFAS to recall all of the product within two years.
Prohibits the sale of medical adhesives and bandages containing PFAS.
Prohibits the sale of cosmetic and personal care products with intentionally added PFAS substances.

Toxic Chemical: Heavy Metals, Formaldehyde, Toxic Chemicals General, Toxic Flame Retardants, Phthalates, PFAS, Bisphenols
Issue/Sector: Packaging Reduction, PVC, Polystyrene, Plastics, Packaging
Description: Require companies selling or distributing packaging materials and products to register with a packaging reduction organization to develop a packaging reduction and recycling plan. Prohibits toxic substances in packaging, including ortho-phthalates, bisphenols, PFAS, heavy meals, halogenated flame retardants, formaldehyde, polyvinyl chloride, polystyrene, and more.

Toxic Chemical: Formaldehyde, Toxic Chemicals General, Lead, Mercury, Phthalates, PFAS, Cadmium
Description: Requires disclosure of toxic chemicals in pet products.
Establishes grant and rebate programs to support the installation and maintenance of PFAS removal treatments for private well users.

Prohibits sale, manufacture, distribution, and use of firefighting foam containing intentionally added perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances. Requires DEP to establish a collection and disposal program.
Requires the New York Department of Environmental Conservation to classify PFAS as high toxicity air contaminant.
Prohibits the sale of anti-fogging products containing PFAS.

Requires the Department of Environmental Protection and the Drinking Water Quality Institute to perform a study concerning the regulation and treatment of perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.
Requires PFAS monitoring and disclosure for industrial dischargers and publicly owned treatment works .
Establishes a five-year moratorium on biosolid land application to manage PFAS contamination.
Requires truthful labeling of plastic recyclability. Products containing PFAS will not be considered recyclable.
Prohibits the sale of playground surfacing materials containing intentionally added PFAS, PAHs, or lead above a certain level starting January 1, 2027.

Prohibits the sale of consumer and household products containing PFAS, including textile articles, rugs, fabric treatments, cookware, ski waxes, architectural paints, cleaning products, and dental floss.
Appropriations bill. Includes up to $500,000 to the State University of New York at Stony Brook to provide for a new laboratory testing facility for PFOA and other chemicals.
Requires certain health insurance policies and social services programs in New York to cover blood testing for PFAS when ordered by a licensed healthcare provider.
Prohibits the sale and manufacture of photovoltaic modules containing PFAS.
Requires the state to set a timeline and safety standard to phase out PFAS in turnout gear starting in 2028.

Issue/Sector: Textiles, Children's Products, Personal Care Products, Packaging, Cleaning Products
Description: Prohibits intentionally added PFAS in consumer products including food packaging, carpets and rugs, cleaning products, cookware, cosmetics, dental floss, fabric treatments, juvenile products, menstruation products, textile furnishings, ski wax, and upholstered furniture. Requires manufacturers to submit detailed information about PFAS content, purposes, and testing results.

Requires a drinking water management plan for private or public schools. Plans must include water sampling and testing
at each filtered bottle-filling station and filtered faucet in the school for lead, PFOS, and PFOA.
Establishes a municipal PFAS grant program for PFAS testing at municipal water systems and municipal wastewater treatment facilities.

Toxic Chemical: PFAS, Mercury, Toxic Chemicals General, Lead, Triclosan, Phthalates, Formaldehyde
Description: Prohibits certain unsafe chemicals in menstrual products such as lead, mercury, formaldehyde, triclosan, phthalates, parabens, and PFAS.
Allows certain parties of releases and covenants involving PFAS to seek contribution from other responsible parties.
Prohibits the use, manufacturing, sale, and distribution of pesticides containing PFAS.
Prohibits any person from manufacturing, distributing, selling, or offering for sale in the state any menstrual products that contain regulated PFAS.
Prohibits menstrual products with intentionally added PFAS. Requires manufacturers to test their products for PFAS.

Authorizes the New York state environmental facilities corporation to award grants and loans to non-public entities for projects focused on improving water quality by removing PFAS from public water systems.
Provides for a partial real property tax exemption for the PFAS water filtration plant for the City of Newburgh.
Requires written notice of certain firefighting equipment containing chemicals. Prohibits sale, manufacture, and distribution of certain firefighting equipment containing intentionally added PFAS.
Requires notification for counties and tribes of an exceedance of groundwater protection standards or standards for PFAS.
Includes a provision restricting PFAS in firefighting personal protective equipment.

Beginning January 1, 2023, bans the use of PFAS chemicals in paper-based food packaging and, beginning January 1, 2024, requires disclosure of toxic substances in cookware, and ensures that a company isn’t misleading consumers by claiming that cookware is free of a particular toxin when it contains a similar compound in the same chemical class.

Reforms labeling for compostable products, ensuring that an item that claims to be compostable actually is so. The law will also cut chemical contamination of compost by banning PFAS from any products labeled as compostable.
Appropriations bill. Includes $50 million to address perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in drinking water systems.
Appropriations bill. Includes $30 million to address perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in drinking water systems.

Safer Solution: Promoting Safer Solutions, Phasing Out the Bad
Description: Restricts PFAS in apparel (both indoor and outdoor), accessories, and handbags starting in 2025. PFAS in outdoor apparel for severe wet conditions will be eliminated as of 2028 and manufacturers will be required to disclose the presence of PFAS in the products as of 2025. The law also directs manufacturers to use the least toxic alternative as they move away from PFAS.

Toxic Chemical: Heavy Metals, PFAS, Toxic Flame Retardants, Phthalates, Toxic Chemicals General, Bisphenols
Description: Establishes a process to identify, prioritize and evaluate chemicals of concern in consumer products, determine how best to limit exposure or reduce the level of hazard, and establishes green chemistry challenge grants and a Green Ribbon Science Panel.

Toxic Chemical: Toxic Chemicals General, PFAS, Mercury, Formaldehyde, Phthalates
Description: Bans select chemicals in cosmetic products (specific phthalates, parabens, phenylenediamines, formaldehyde, mercury, and 13 PFAS).
Restricts perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in cosmetic products. Effective January 1, 2025.
Beginning July 1, 2023, bans the entire class of PFAS from a wide array of “juvenile” products including booster seats, changing pads, infant carriers, nursing pillows and crib mattresses.
Requires public water systems to monitor for PFAS.

Prohibits the use of firefighting foam containing PFAS; establishes standards for the disposal of firefighting foam containing PFAS; establishes a working group to study issues related to PFAS and develop recommendations for state action; prohibits any flame retardant organohalogenated chemical in the manufacture, sale, and distribution of children’s products, upholstered residential furniture, residential textiles, business textiles, or mattresses (specifically exempts polymeric flame retardants).
Requires state department to conduct a study on microplastics and PFAS in food packaging and food waste.
Appropriations bill. Includes allocating $420,000 for the purchase of laboratory equipment to test for PFAS in drinking water to support public health testing requirements
Appropriates $550k to improve public water systems contaminated with PFAS, and $50k to reimburse schools with contaminated water who must utilize alternate water supplies.
Allocates funding and establishes new programs to enhance water infrastructure. Includes funding for PFAS testing and remediation.
Prohibits the use and sale of toxic sludge generated from municipal, commercial, or industrial wastewater treatment plants.
Appropriations bill. Provides $3 million to the Department of Environmental Conservation for PFAS remediation.

Issue/Sector: Textiles, Packaging, Cleaning Products, Personal Care Products, Children's Products, Artificial Turf, Firefighting Foam
Description: Prohibits the manufacture, sale, or distribution for sale of consumer products containing PFAS including firefighting foam, artificial turf, carpets or rugs, cleaning products, cookware, cosmetic products, cookware, dental loss, food packaging, incontinence products, juvenile products, menstrual products, ski wax, and upholstered furniture.

Requires written notice for PFAS in firefighting gear beginning July 1, 2026. Prohibits the sale or distribution of any personal protective equipment or station wear with intentionally added PFAS beginning July 1, 2028.
Establishes a fund to support the development of PFAS-free firefighter gear and other protective materials.

Requires setting maximum contaminant levels for specific PFAS in drinking water, with a zero parts per trillion limit for certain PFAS and a 20 parts per trillion limit for others, effective July 1, 2025.
Prohibits the land application, sale, and landfill disposal of biosolids and similar wastes containing PFAS chemicals.
Prohibits the land application of septage, sludge, and biosolids, as well as the sale of compost or other agricultural products containing or produced from these materials
Requires a regional management plan for septage, sludge, and biosolids, focusing on safe disposal, cost-effectiveness, and minimizing health risks.
Requires truth in labeling of recycable and compostable products and packaging.
Prohibits the use and sale of pesticides and packaging containing PFAS.

Toxic Chemical: Toxic Chemicals General, PFAS, Phthalates, Lead, Cadmium, Formaldehyde, Heavy Metals, Bisphenols
Description: Prohibits the use of foam and solid polystyrene in disposable food service ware and packing materials. Restricts the provision of single-use disposable products. Sets a timeline for the removal of toxic substances from beverage containers, food ware, and food packaging
Establishes voucher program for residential PFAS filtration equipment.

Toxic Chemical: Heavy Metals, Cadmium, Toxic Flame Retardants, Mercury, PFAS, Lead, Toxic Chemicals General, Bisphenols, Phthalates, Formaldehyde
Issue/Sector: Packaging, Polystyrene, Plastics, Packaging Reduction, PVC
Description: Establishes a Packaging Reduction and Recycling Program. Prohibits toxic substances in packaging, including phthalates, bisphenols, PFAS, lead, cadmium, mercury, halogenated flame retardants, and more. Prohibits packaging containing polyvinyl chloride, polystyrene, or polycarbonate.
Requires municipalities to meet safe drinking water standards for state funding eligibility.
Establishes an ecologically-based mosquito management program. Prohibits aerial applications and products containing PFAS.

Toxic Chemical: PFAS, Formaldehyde, Triclosan, Lead, Mercury, Phthalates
Safer Solution: Promoting Safer Solutions, Phasing Out the Bad
Description: Beginning January 1, 2025, no person may manufacture, knowingly sell, offer for sale, distribute for sale, or distribute for use in this state any cosmetic product that contains any of the following intentionally added chemicals or chemical classes: ortho-phthalates, perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances, formaldehyde, mercury, triclosan, and more.

Requires industrial users of publicly owned treatment works that receive and clean, repair, refurbish, or process items that contain PFAS to test wastestreams for PFAS prior to and after cleaning, repairing, refurbishing, or processing such items.

Requires the Department of Agriculture to study the use of perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in pesticides and requires the Department to report its findings and recommendations on or before November 1, 2023.

Establishes the Innovative Potable Reuse Pilot Program in the Department of the Environment for the purpose of authorizing the regulated use of reclaimed water as a source for certain drinking water facilities. The reclaimed water must meet maximum concentrations of PFAS chemicals established by the department.

Issue/Sector: Cleaning Products, Building Materials/Construction, Personal Care Products, Packaging, Textiles
Description: Prohibits the manufacture, sale, or distribution of certain products containing intentionally added PFAS including cleaning products, cookware, cosmetics, personal care products, feminine hygiene products, pet food packaging, juvenile products, intimacy products, fabric treatments, ski wax, textiles, textile furnishings, upholstered furniture, or paint.
Authorizes a locality to require testing and monitoring for PFAS when sewage sludge is land applied within its jurisdiction.

Requires the department of environmental management to adopt maximum contaminant levels for PFAS chemicals and effluent limitation standards for wastewater containing PFAS chemicals by July 1, 2027. Establishes the PFAS chemical testing and remediation fund. Creates a PFAS chemical grant program.

Extends the duration of a commission investigating the environmental and public health impacts of perfluorinated chemical releases in Merrimack, Bedford, Londonderry, Hudson, and Litchfield, New Hampshire, for an additional five years.
Requires a study on PFAS chemicals in biosolids.

Toxic Chemical: Formaldehyde, Lead, Mercury, PFAS, Phthalates, Toxic Chemicals General, Triclosan
Description: Requires disclosure of all ingredients in menstrual products. Prohibits certain substances in menstrual products including lead, mercury, formaldehyde, triclosan, phthalates, and PFAS.

Prohibits playground surfacing materials that contain a certain concentration of lead or a component product, material, or substance to which PFAS chemicals were previously intentionally added for certain purposes.

Requires the Department of the Environment to identify significant industrial users that currently and intentionally use PFAS chemicals by July 1, 2026; requires the Department to develop certain PFAS monitoring and testing criteria for certain significant industrial users by January 1, 2025; requires the Department by June 1, 2025, to develop PFAS action levels for addressing PFAS contamination from industrial discharge for pretreatment permits.
Establishes a presumption of disability for firefighters diagnosed with health conditions related to PFAS.
Establishes a PFAS biomonitoring pilot program under the department of homeland security to collect and analyze blood samples of individuals who were previously, or are currently, firefighters.

Toxic Chemical: Phthalates, PFAS, Formaldehyde, Toxic Chemicals General, Mercury
Description: Prohibits the manufacture, sale, or delivery of cosmetic products containing certain ingredients including phthalates, formaldehyde, mercury, certain PFAS, and more.

Safer Solution: Phasing Out the Bad, Safer Management Systems
Description: Establishes producer responsibility regulations. Includes restrictions on certain addtives in packaging including lead, cadmium, mercury, PFAS, and phthalates.
Requires the Indiana department of health to establish state maximum contaminant levels for PFAS in water.
Prohibits the manufacture, distribution, or sale of firefighting foam containing PFAS. Requires labeling of firefighting personal equipment containing PFAS.
Creates the agricultural PFAS remediation fund in order to assist farmers in New Hampshire. Imposes a 5 year moratorium on the spreading and application of sludge and biosolids for agricultural use.

Requires disclosure of products containing intentionally added PFAS. Prohibits intentionally added PFAS in certain products such as carpets, cookware, cosmetics, and food packaging. Prohibits the sale of any product with intentionally added PFAS  unless deemed currently unavoidable by the Pollution Control Board.
Beginning June 30, 2024, prohibits fire departments from purchasing firefighter gear unless it contains a label indicating whether or not the firefighter gear contains a PFAS chemical.

Safer Solution: Phasing Out the Bad, Promoting Safer Solutions, Transparency
Description: Requires disclosure of firefighting personal protective equipment containing PFAS chemicals. Encourages the prioritization of PFAS-free equipment purchases. Beginning January 1, 2025, prohibits the provision of equipment containing intentionally added PFAS.
Requires testing for PFAS in public water systems.

Toxic Chemical: Phthalates, Formaldehyde, PFAS, Toxic Chemicals General
Description: Provides that a person who violates the prohibition on the manufacture, or sale of a cosmetic product that contains certain toxic chemicals is liable for certain civil damages.
Establishes a PFAS working group. Requires reporting from manufacturers that intentionally added PFAS in products.
Requires monitoring and reduction of PFAS levels in the Occoquan Reservoir by facilities discharging industrial wastewater.
Prohibits the use of PFAS in certain consumer products including cosmetics, dental floss, juvenile products, and menstrual products.
Requires annual testing of drinking water from all public water systems for PFAS. Businesses involved in the production, use, or disposal of PFAS may face potential liability costs.

Issue/Sector: Children's Products, Cleaning Products, Personal Care Products, Textiles
Description: Prohibits the sale or distribution of products containing intentionally added PFAS including carpets, cleaning products, cookware, cosmetics, dental floss, fabric treatments, juvenile products, menstruation products, textile furnishings, ski wax, and upholstered furniture. By January 1, 2033, any product containing intentionally added PFAS will be banned unless deemed unavoidable
Requires disclosure of PFAS in firefighting personal protective equipment (PPE). Prohibits the manufacture, sale, or distribution of firefighting PPE with intentionally added PFAS.

Issue/Sector: Textiles, Children's Products, Personal Care Products, Packaging
Description: Prohibits the sale or distribution of certain products containing intentionally added PFAS including cookware, cosmetics, dental floss, juvenile products, menstrual products, intimate apparel, or food packaging or food contact products.
Establishes maximum contaminant levels for certain PFAS and other pollutants in drinking water.
Allocates $1 million for a health study on PFAS levels in blood from contaminated drinking water sources.
Prohibits the use of class B firefighting foam with intentionally added PFAS chemicals.
Requires testing of PFAS in biosolids. Prohibits spreading of biosolids under certain circumstances.
Directs Oregon State University to study PFAS in biosolids on non-food agricultural fields.
Establishes a fund and tax to manage PFAS-related costs and environmental impacts.
Establishes a committee to monitor and recommend policies for PFAS mitigation in wastewater systems, holding manufacturers responsible for costs.

Requires Department of Environmental Services to establish regulations that prohibit the discharge of specific chemicals, particularly perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), into the state’s waterways and drinking water supplies.

Establishes the Oklahoma Biosolids Land Application Research Pilot Program at Oklahoma State University. The program aims to research the use of biosolids in agriculture, including the development of application protocols, agronomic studies, and assessments of environmental impacts.

Toxic Chemical: Toxic Chemicals General, Mercury, Formaldehyde, PFAS, Phthalates
Description: Prohibits the sale and manufacture of cosmetics with certain harmful chemicals including phthalates, formaldehyde, certain PFAS chemicals, and more.
Requires the Department of Environmental Protection to adopt drinking water quality standards for lead and PFAS that meet or exceed federal Environmental Protection Agency standards.
Establishes the Residential Well Water Testing and Treatment Program and Program Fund to test and treat contaminated drinking water.

Establishes a take-back program for fire departments that use and store firefighting foam containing PFAS chemicals. The program shall provide funding and resources to ensure the proper disposal or destruction of firefighting foam containing PFAS.
Prohibits the use of PFAS in firefighting foam and requires labeling for personal protective equipment containing PFAS.
Requires all firefighters, including volunteer firefighters, undergo annual testing for the presence of PFAS substances in their bloodstreams.

Directs the department of environmental services and the department of health and human services to enter into a memorandum of understanding on cooperation regarding data sharing. Changes the reporting requirement from 6 months to an annual basis and requires it to be submitted to the commission on the environmental and public health impacts of PFAS.

Toxic Chemical: Mercury, Lead, PFAS, Toxic Chemicals General
Description: Requires manufacturers of menstrual and feminine hygiene products to disclose certain chemicals and intentionally added ingredients on product packaging, including substances such as lead, cadmium, arsenic, and PFAS.
Appropriations bill. Provides $39 million to address PFAS and emerging contaminants.
Establishes a pilot program to support research on PFAS exposure, particularly for individuals who have been affected by contaminated water supplies.
Establishes a grant program aimed at enabling research institutions to conduct a pilot program that assesses the levels and effects of PFAS in the blood of qualified participants.
Prohibit pesticides with intentionally added PFAS, mandating disclosure.
Appropriations bill. Includes $918,000 for a PFAS foam reclamation project.
Prohibits pesticides containing PFAS and other toxic chemicals within 1000 feet of a school.
Requires annual blood testing for PFAS and cancer screenings for all active career and volunteer firefighters at no cost to them.
Prohibits the sale of firefighting personal protective equipment containing intentionally added PFAS as of January 1, 2027.
Requires testing and monitoring of wastewater discharges including biosolids for PFAS substances.
Requires testing of PFAS substances in drinking water.

Safer Solution: Phasing Out the Bad, Promoting Safer Solutions
Description: Prohibits the use of polystyrene containers by restaurants and the provision of single-use food service items unless requested by a consumer. Prohibits the distribution of single-use straws that contain PFAS.
Requires PFAS manufacturers to cover costs for public water systems’ PFAS contamination.
Prohibits the use of firefighting foams containing PFAS chemicals for training, practice, or testing purposes.
Requires quarterly testing of biosolids for PFAS contaminants by those seeking to apply biosolids to lands, with subsequent reports to the department of environmental management.

Provides that water supply systems that have PFAS contaminants that exceed the interim drinking water standard be required to enter into a consent agreement with department of health to implement treatment to reduce the levels of PFAS contaminants.

Appropriations bill. Includes $1,806,900 in fiscal year 2024-2025 and $1,207,600 infiscal year 2025-2026 to support additional personnel and operating costs associated with the analysis and regulation of per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances.
Requires publicly owned treatment works to collect information from industrial users about PFAS discharges.
Prohibits the use, sale, or distribution of disposable or single-use plastic food ware and beverage service items, including those containing PFAS.
Prohibits the discharge of leachate from landfills and solid waste management facilities into the watershed of Lake Memphremagog.
Prohibits the sale, manufacture, and distribution of firefighting personal protective equipment containing PFAS chemicals.

Toxic Chemical: Phthalates, Toxic Chemicals General, Formaldehyde, Heavy Metals, PFAS
Description: Prohibits the sale of cosmetics with certain restricted substances including PFAS, heavy metals, parabens, phthalates, formaldehyde, benzophenones, asbestos, butylated compounds, siloxanes, phenylenediamines, and nitrosamines.
Appropriations bill. Includes $13.7 million for a PFAS removal water treatment plant.
Appropriations bill. Includes $11.6 million for a PFAS removal water treatment plant.
Requires data centers to monitor and test PFAS in wastewater. Prohibits the addition of PFAS to water discharge from data centers.

Issue/Sector: Cleaning Products, Personal Care Products, Children's Products, Textiles
Description: Prohibits intentionally added PFAS in consumer products including carpets, cleaning products, cookware, cosmetics, juvenile items, and upholstered furniture. Requires manufacturers to submit detailed information about PFAS content, purposes, and testing results.

Toxic Chemical: PFAS, Lead, Toxic Chemicals General, Cadmium
Description: Updates the existing Toxic Packaging Act by delaying the ban on PFAS in food packaging until January 1, 2025, and in processing agents until July 1, 2027. Includes a prohibition on lead, cadmium, mercury, hexavalent chromium, and PFAS in packaging and packaging components.
Establishes maximum contaminant levels for PFAS, PFOA, and PFOS in drinking water.
Prohibits the manufacture and sale of cosmetic products containing certain toxic chemicals, including phthalates, formaldehyde, mercury, certain PFAS, and more.
Requires owners of sewage treatment facilities to conduct monthly sampling and testing of sewage sludge for PFAS.

Safer Solution: Phasing Out the Bad, Setting The Stage
Description: Requires monitoring of PFAS levels at firefighter-related sites and prohibits the use of firefighting equipment containing PFAS as an intentionally added chemical after July 1, 2030. Establishes the Firefighter Health Advisory Council to monitor health risks and develop the Firefighter PFAS Initiative, which includes PFAS blood testing for firefighters.
Prohibits land application of sludge containing certain PFAS substances.
Packaging for cannabis flower, cannabinoid products, and hemp-derived consumer​ products must not contain or be coated with any perfluoroalkyl substance.

Issue/Sector: Firefighting Foam, Water, Personal Care Products, Cleaning Products, Textiles
Safer Solution: Transparency, Accountability, Phasing Out the Bad
Description: Appropriates funding to address PFAS contamination. Restricts all unnecessary PFAS uses by 2032, bans 13 product categories specific uses, and requires companies to disclose presence of PFAS in any product. Prohibits the sale, distribution, and use of class B firefighting foam containing PFAS chemicals. Requires Pollution Control Agency to establish water quality standards for certain PFAS chemicals.
Allocates $40 million for improvements to Apple Valley’s water treatment plant specifically to address PFAS contaminants.
Allocates $1.6 million for water infrastructure improvements specifically targeting PFAS removal from drinking water in Oak Park Heights.
Allocates $1,075,000 for PFAS biomonitoring in eastern metropolitan communities.
Allocates $5.5 million for a demonstration project to treat landfill leachate for PFAS and other contaminants.
Allocates funds for a demonstration project to treat landfill leachate for PFAS and other contaminants.

Issue/Sector: Cleaning Products, Textiles, Personal Care Products, Children's Products
Description: Prohibits the use, manufacture, distribution, and sale of consumer products containing PFAS substances including carpets/rgs, cleaning products, cookware, cosmetics, dental loss, fabric treatments, juvenile products, menstruation products, textile furnishings, ski wax, and upholstered furniture. Prohibits the the sale of any product with intentionally added PFAS unless deemed an unavoidable use. Requires manufacturers to submit detailed information about products containing intentionally added PFAS.
Requires wastewater treatment plants to test sewage sludge for PFAS before it is used on farmland and allows landowners to refuse its application if PFAS are detected.
Imposes a tax on PFAS product manufacturers and retailers in Minnesota, establishes a cleanup fund for environmental purposes, and creates an advisory commission to oversee fund usage.

Establishes the Agricultural PFAS Relief Fund to assist farmers with costs related to PFAS contamination. Establishes the Agricultural Fertilizer Purchasing Fund to supports farmers facing increased fertilizer costs due to PFAS-related restrictions.

Issue/Sector: Personal Care Products, Children's Products, Textiles, Packaging, Biosolids/Sludge, Firefighting Foam
Description: Establishes a PFAS Remediation Trust Fund to support remediation efforts and regulatory compliance. Prohibits PFAS in consumer products, food packaging, and firefighting foam and requires manufacturers to report products containing PFAS. Requires the Department of Environmental Protection to create regulations to phase out the use of sludge and to monitor PFAS in ambient air.

Prohibits PFAS and high priority chemicals of concern in children’s products. Requires the Department of Environmental Protection to maintain and update a publicly accessible list of toxic chemicals of concern.

Prohibits the manufacture, use, and distribution of PFAS substances. Requires full disclosure of pollutants in discharge applications and requires the elimination of PFAS and 1,4-dioxane from wastewater.

Toxic Chemical: Cadmium, Formaldehyde, Heavy Metals, PFAS, Toxic Flame Retardants, Mercury, Lead, Bisphenols, Phthalates, Toxic Chemicals General
Issue/Sector: Plastics, Packaging, PVC, Polystyrene, Packaging Reduction
Safer Solution: Safer Management Systems, Phasing Out the Bad
Description: Establishes extended producer responsibility for packaging materials, prohibits certain toxic substances in packaging, and prohibit intentionally added PFAS in covered products.

Toxic Chemical: Cadmium, Mercury, PFAS, Lead, Toxic Chemicals General
Issue/Sector: Textiles, Firefighting Foam, Disposal/Incineration, Firefighter Gear, Packaging
Description: Restricts the use of PFAS chemicals in food packaging, rugs and carpets, and replaces PFAS-laden firefighting foam with safer alternatives. Requires notification for firefighter turnout gear that contains PFAS and stops the landfilling and incineration of PFAS foam.

Toxic Chemical: Mercury, Phthalates, Formaldehyde, PFAS, Toxic Chemicals General
Description: Bans 13 different types of PFAS, 2 types of phthalates, mercury, formaldehyde, and other chemicals of concern in the manufacture, sale, and delivery of cosmetics.

A 2021-2022 appropriations bill that includes providing $5,950,000 to the City of DuPont for PFAS treatment, $5,569,000 to Lakewood Water District for a PFAS Treatment Facility, and $1,150,00 for a PFAS Pilot Project by the Department of Ecology.
Enables wastewater treatment plants to require providers of discharge to test such discharge for PFAS.
Prohibits the use of PFAS-containing firefighting foam for training purposes or testing purposes.
Requires Department of Health to set Maximum Contaminant Levels for PFAS and other chemicals in drinking water.
Sets Maximum Contaminant Levels for PFOA, PFOS, PFHxS, and PFNA in drinking water; establishes PFAS fund.

Restricts the sale and distribution of consumer products containing PFAS chemicals. Consumer products include oil and gas products; carpets or rugs; cosmetics; fabric treatments; food packaging; juvenile products; textile furnishings; and upholstered furniture.

Establishes a regulatory scheme that requires disclosure of certain chemical information for products used in downhole oil and gas operations. The discloser must also provide the commission with a declaration that the chemical product contains no intentionally added perfluoroalkyl or polyfluoroalkyl chemicals.
Creates remediation fund for customers of water utilities financially impacted by PFAS contamination related to military installations.
Requires the Department of Environmental Protection to adopt statewide rules for cleanup target levels for perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in soil and groundwater by a specified date.
Allows the commissioner of the department of environmental services to adopt rules about airborne PFAS in certain circumstances.
Requires the commissioner of the department of environmental services to adopt rules relative to perfluorinated chemical remediation in the soil.

Beginning December 31, 2024, prohibits the manufacture, sale, distribution, and use of food packaging that contain perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances. Beginning July 1, 2024 prohibits the manufacture, sale, distribution, and use of class B firefighting foam that contains intentionally added PFAS for training and testing purposes.
Improves logistical processes for the regulation of priority chemicals in consumer products.

Prohibits the sale of PFAS-containing firefighting foam in certain circumstances, prohibits the use of PFAS foam for training, requires manufacturers to disclose if protective equipment they produce includes PFAS, and requires the Department of Health to conduct a survey to determine the amount of PFAS foam currently held, used, and disposed by fire departments.

Addresses when PFAS may be used for firefighting foam system testing both in general and in certain aircraft hangars. Requires the solid and hazardous waste commission to promulgate rules for both a certificate of registration for any facility, fire department, or lessee subject to federal rules and regulations that uses or stores PFAS in its operations and for standards for the capture and disposal of PFAS. Prohibits the use of class B firefighting foam that contains intentionally added PFAS in certain aircraft hangars beginning January 1, 2023.
Eliminates exemptions from restrictions on use of PFAS-containing firefighting foam.
This bill creates a statute of limitation (6 years) on civil actions relative to damage caused by perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.

Adds members from Londonderry to the commission to investigate and analyze the environmental and public health impacts relating to releases of perfluorinated chemicals into the air, soil, and groundwater in Merrimack, Bedford, and Litchfield.

Safer Solution: Promoting Safer Solutions, Phasing Out the Bad
Description: Prohibits the manufacture, sale, and distribution of food packaging containing PFAS chemicals and requires the Department of Ecology to conduct an assessment on safer alternatives.
Directs the department of environmental services to set maximum contaminant limits for certain per and polyfluoroalkyl substances.

Prohibits the discharge of class B firefighting foam that contains intentionally added PFAS chemicals unless such discharge occurs in fire prevention or in response to an emergency firefighting operation.
Requires discharges of PFAS-containing firefighting foam to be reported to the state. Establishes a take-back program for PFAS foams.
Prohibits the use of PFAS firefighting foam for training purposes.
Prohibits the use of PFAS-containing firefighting foam for training purposes or testing purposes.
Prohibits the disposal by incineration of perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances listed in the USEPA’s Toxic Release Inventory, including, but not limited to, aqueous film-forming foam.
Appropriates $1 million to assist homeowners with private well water contaminated with PFOA and/or PFOS.
Appropriations bill. Includes $29.6 million for the testing and remediation of any pollutant that is a perfluoroalkyl or polyfluoroalkyl substance.
Appropriations bill. Provides $2 million for PFAS cleanup and remediation.
Appropriations bill. Includes $500,000 for disposal of firefighting foam containing PFAS, $20 million for PFAS and environmental contamination response, and $3 million for PFAS remediation.
Tasks Commissioner of Health to convene work group to study occurrence of PFAS in state drinking water and develop recommendations for Maximum Contaminant Levels.
Prohibits the use of PFAS-containing firefighting foam for training purposes or testing purposes.
Requires water bottler companies to test the water source they are bottling for PFAS. Effective January 1, 2022.
Provides up to $1,150,000 in grants-in-aid to municipalities for the purpose of providing potable water and for assessment and remedial action to address PFAS pollution. Effective July 1, 2021.

Authorizes the department of health to take action to establish maximum contaminant levels for per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances in drinking water and set interim standards. The act would also provide that the department of environmental management set standards for PFAS in ground and surface waters, and adopt standards for PFAS monitoring at landfills.

Establishes a commission to investigate and analyze the environmental and public health impacts relating to releases of perfluorinated chemicals in the air, soil, and groundwater in Merrimack, Bedford and Litchfield.
Prohibits the sale or promotional distribution of any food package in Rhode Island which contains perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances, effective January 1, 2024.
Directs the Department of Natural Resources and Environmental Control and the Division of Public Health to establish maximum contaminant levels for PFOA and PFOS.

Requires the Board of Health to adopt maximum contaminant levels in all water supplies and waterworks in the Commonwealth for perfluorooctanoic acid, perfluorooctane sulfonate, and for such other perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.

Toxic Chemical: Phthalates, Toxic Chemicals General, Toxic Flame Retardants, PFAS, Bisphenols, Heavy Metals
Description: Generates a list of chemicals of high concern and priority chemicals, along with participation in Interstate Chemicals Clearinghouse.
Appropriations bill. Includes funding for for assessing mercury and other fish contaminants, including PFAS.

Bans manufacture and sale of halogenated, phosphorus-based, nitrogen-based, and nanoscale flame retardants in residential upholstered furniture, children’s products, and residential and business textiles. Prohibits the manufacture and sale of PFAS-containing firefighting foam.
Appropriates funds to research and address PFAS contamination.
Establishes PFAS Response Program to respond to and address PFAS contamination affecting agricultural producers.
Promotes economic reuse of PFAS contaminated farmland through the Clean Energy Department.
Appropriations bill. Provides one-time funding to replace firefighting gear that is known to have perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.
Extends deadline for manufacturers to notify Maine DEP of their products for sale in the state that contain intentionally added PFAS from January 1, 2023 to January 1, 2025.
Allocates $10 million to the Kennebec Water District to expand water infrastructure in Fairfield, Maine, addressing PFAS contamination in private wells.

Requires bottlers to conduct testing for PFAS. Prohibits the sale of bottled water that contains a regulated PFAS contaminant in an amount that exceeds the standard applicable to a community water system.

Safer Solution: Setting The Stage, Phasing Out the Bad
Description: Sets maximum PFAS levels in drinking water for certain Maine water systems, mandates monitoring and treatment if levels are exceeded, and requires reporting and notification procedures.

Issue/Sector: Personal Care Products, Children's Products, Cleaning Products, Artificial Turf, Textiles
Description: Amends existing PFAS law. Beginning January 1, 2026, prohibits PFAS in cleaning products, cookware, cosmetics, dental floss, juvenile products, menstrual products, textile articles, ski wax, and upholstered furniture. Beginning January 1, 2029, prohibits PFAS in artificial turf, outdoor apparel for extreme weather conditions, and certain refrigerants. Requires disclosure by manufacturers who seek an exemption from the ban on PFAS in all products beginning in 2032 unless they ask for a currently unavoidable use designation.
Establishes regulations concerning the management and testing of landfill leachate for PFAS chemicals.
Requires monitoring, control, and treatment and reduction of PFAS in landfill leachate and emissions of sludge treatment facilities.

Changes the name of the Land Application Contaminant Monitoring Fund to the Well Contamination Response Fund. Provides funding for private drinking water wells impacted by hazardous substances including PFAS.
Establishes a take-back and disposal program for firefighting foam containing PFAS.
Requires an inventory of aqueous film-forming foam concentrate held by both public and private entities in the state.
Provides tenants the right to know if the water coming from their household wells is contaminated with PFAS.

Requires free testing for contaminants in private wells of low-income residents, funded by a dedicated account, and expands grant eligibility for water treatment systems, while requiring educational outreach to increase awareness.
Provides impacted people access to PFAS blood testing through their insurance carrier, helping them monitor and address impacts of PFAS to their health.

Toxic Chemical: Heavy Metals, PFAS, Phthalates, Toxic Chemicals General, Toxic Flame Retardants, Bisphenols
Description: Selects up to seventy chemicals as Chemicals of High Concern based upon likely exposure to children or fetuses, and uses this list to designate Priority Chemicals which will require reporting and disclosure when used in children’s products.
Requires PFAS monitoring in drinking water and directs the Department of Health ad Human Services to establish maximum contaminant levels for PFAS substances by June 1, 2024.
Prohibits the sale of food packaging with intentionally added toxic heavy metals, PFAS, or phthalates.

Issue/Sector: Textiles, Packaging, Personal Care Products, Cleaning Products
Description: Requires manufacturers to report products containing intentionally added PFAS beginning in 2023. Prohibits the sale of residential carpets or rugs, and fabric treatments, that contain intentionally added PFAS beginning in 2023. Prohibits the sale of products containing intentionally added PFAS beginning in 2030 unless the use of PFAS in a product is specifically designated as “currently unavoidable”.
Prohibits the use, manufacture, sale, and distribution of firefighting foam containing PFAS. Effective January 1, 2022.

Creates the Land Application Contaminant Monitoring Fund to test and monitor soil and groundwater for PFAS substances and other contaminants. The fund is funded by a $10 per ton fee assessed on any disposal of septage, industrial sludge, municipal sludge, bioash, wood ash, or other residuals. Also requires the Department of Environmental Protection to test land that has received residuals for PFAS.

Provides funding to expand efforts to address imminent risk to public health through investment in public water system improvements, including mitigation of lead in drinking water at schools and daycares and perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances, or PFAS, effects.

Requires the Department of Environmental Protection to inform the public of the current federal Environmental Protection Agency standards or the state maximum contaminant level for PFAS in drinking water.
Requires state department to study methods of treating leachate from state-owned landfills to reduce the concentration of perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in the leachate.
Prohibits the spreading of PFAS-laden sludge and sludge-derived compost as fertilizer.
Beginning January 1, 2030, bans pesticides that contains intentionally added PFAS.

Amends the law regulating firefighting and fire-suppressing foams to which PFAS have been intentionally added to provide that the prohibition on the manufacture, sale and distribution of such foams does not include foams manufactured, sold or distributed for a marine defense application if the use of the foam is required by the United States Department of Defense.

Toxic Chemical: Toxic Flame Retardants, PFAS, Bisphenols, Toxic Chemicals General, Heavy Metals, Phthalates
Description: Identifies chemicals of high concern, and requires reporting on usage and replacement with safer alternatives.

Appropriations bill. includes $10 million in funding to abate, clean up and mitigate threats or hazards posed by PFAS; $188,000 to fund a program to work directly with affected farmers on PFAS mitigation efforts;  $3 million to establish and fund 17 positions to assist in the identification and management of PFAS; and $16.5 million to for PFAS testing, management, and treatment of drinking water.
Directs the Board of Pesticides Control to gather information relating to perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in pesticides.

Provides that an action arising out of any harm or injury caused by a perfluoroalkyl or polyfluoroalkyl substance must be commenced within 6 years after the plaintiff discovers or should have discovered such harm or injury. It does not alter the 15 notice period or limitation period applicable to claims against a government entity.
Directs the Department of Agriculture, Conservation, and Forestry to study alternative cropping systems for farmers affected by PFAS contamination.
Establishes a cause of action for medical monitoring expenses.

Prohibits the manufacture, sale, distribution, and use of firefighting foam containing intentionally added PFAS beginning October 1, 2023 . Requires manufacturers selling firefighting equipment to disclose if it contains PFAS. Prohibits the manufacture, sale, and distribution of food packaging containing intentionally added PFAS, inks, dyes, pigments, adhesives, stabilizers, coatings, plasticizers, or any other additives to which ortho-phthalates have been intentionally added. Allows the Department of Health to adopt rules prohibiting bisphenols in food packaging. Prohibits PFAS in carpets, rugs, fabric treatments, and ski wax.

Toxic Chemical: Bisphenols, Heavy Metals, Phthalates, PFAS, Toxic Chemicals General
Description: Establishes a process for identifying chemicals of high concern; prohibits sale or distribution of consumer products containing priority chemicals.
Prohibits the manufacture, sale, and distribution for use of firefighting foam containing PFAS.
Sets Maximum Contaminant Levels for 5 PFAS chemicals in water of 20ppt each and cumulatively, requires testing for PFAS chemicals, and requires landfills to treat leachate to remove PFAS chemicals.
Prohibits the manufacture and sale of firefighting foam containing PFAS, requires manufacturers of firefighter protective equipment to disclose the inclusion of PFAS in their products.
Prohibits the use of food packaging containing PFAS chemicals.

State budget included funding for university research to monitor for PFAS in rivers, provisions for alternative water supplies for residents near a factory that has contaminated nearby wells, and funding for studies of downstream impacts.
Prohibits the use of perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in apparel as an intentionally added chemical.

Toxic Chemical: Heavy Metals, Formaldehyde, Toxic Chemicals General, Toxic Flame Retardants, Phthalates, PFAS, Bisphenols
Issue/Sector: Packaging Reduction, PVC, Polystyrene, Plastics, Packaging
Description: Require companies selling or distributing packaging materials and products to register with a packaging reduction organization to develop a packaging reduction and recycling plan. Prohibits toxic substances in packaging, including ortho-phthalates, bisphenols, PFAS, heavy meals, halogenated flame retardants, formaldehyde, polyvinyl chloride, polystyrene, and more.
Requires PFAS to be regulated as a high toxicity air contaminant. Establishes a fence line monitoring program to monitor PFAS emissions in disadvantaged communities.

Issue/Sector: Personal Care Products, Children's Products, Textiles, Packaging, Firefighter Gear, Biosolids/Sludge
Description: Establishes a PFAS Remediation Trust Fund to support remediation efforts and regulatory compliance. Prohibits PFAS in consumer products, food packaging, and firefighting personal protective equipment and requires manufacturers to report products containing PFAS. Requires the Department of Environmental Protection to create regulations to phase out the use of sludge and to monitor PFAS in ambient air.
Prohibits the use of PFAS in food packaging.
Establishes a fund to support the development and procurement of PFAS-free firefighter gear and the disposal of existing gear containing PFAS.

Toxic Chemical: Toxic Chemicals General, Heavy Metals, PFAS
Description: Prohibits state and municipal funding for artificial turf fields containing zinc, plastic, or intentionally-added PFAS.

Toxic Chemical: Bisphenols, Toxic Chemicals General, Lead, Triclosan, PFAS, Formaldehyde, Phthalates
Issue/Sector: Artificial Turf, Personal Care Products, Textiles
Description: Prohibit the manufacture, sale, and distribution of cosmetic and menstrual products containing certain chemicals and chemical classes. Prohibits the manufacture, sale, and distribution in Vermont of textiles containing perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances. Prohibits the installationof any new athletic turf field containing perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.
Authorizes spending for infrastructure improvements, environmental protection, and local economic support. Includes $385 million for addressing PFAS contamination through the Clean Water Trust.
Requires notice for PFAS in firefighter PPE starting January 1, 2025. Prohibits PFAS-containing firefighting PPE starting January 1, 2027.

Toxic Chemical: Heavy Metals, PFAS, Toxic Chemicals General, Mercury
Description: Requires testing of private well water for certain contaminants.
Prohibits the sale of cosmetic and personal care products with intentionally added PFAS.
Establishes maximum contaminant levels for certain PFAS chemicals in drinking water.
Requires the Commission for Public Health in North Carolina to establish maximum contaminant levels for certain chemicals in drinking water including PFAS, PFOA, PFOS, chromium-6, and 1,4-dioxane.
Requires manufacturers to recall and safely dispose of class B firefighting foam with added PFAS.
Imposes a six-month moratorium on synthetic turf installations in New York, pending a study on health and environmental risks.
Prohibits playground surfacing materials that contain PFAS, PAHs or lead. Requires manufactuers to disclose playground surfacing materials that contain PFAS, PAHs or lead.
Establishes grant and rebate programs to support the installation and maintenance of PFAS removal treatments for private well users.
Requires PFAS monitoring and disclosure for industrial dischargers.

Prohibits the use of perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances in industrial plants, which are commonly found in items such as vats, conveyor belts, molds, ovens, pans, pots, and chemicals used as mold releasers, lubricants, seals, and coatings.
Establishes an ecologically-based mosquito management program. Prohibits use of larvicides or adulticides containing PFAS.
Allocates funding and establishes new programs to enhance water infrastructure. Includes funding for PFAS testing and remediation.

Establishes a moratorium on biosolid land application, mandates PFAS testing, creates a response program and fund for affected farms, and forms a task force to evaluate PFAS risks and disposal methods.
Imposes a 36-month moratorium on artificial turf installations to address public health and environmental concerns associated with artificial surfaces, particularly the contamination caused by PFAS.
Amends New York’s environmental conservation law to fund restoration projects, redefines the definition of “contaminant” to include hazardous waste, petroleum, certain chemicals, and PFAS substances.

Requires the Director of the Division of Fire Safety to submit a report on the projected cost for the state to fund cancer screenings for all career and volunteer firefighters and replace all personal protective equipment.

Issue/Sector: Children's Products, Textiles, Packaging, Personal Care Products
Description: Prohibits certain products containing intentionally added PFAS, including cookware, cosmetics, dental floss, juvenile products, menstrual products, intimate apparel, and food packaging or food contact products.
Prohibits the sale of juvenile products with intentionally added PFAS.
Appropriations bill. Includes $19.5 million for PFAS contamination response.
Establishes an account in the general fund to provide grants to towns that need PFAS testing and remediation.
Appropriations bill. Provides $90 million for technical and financial assistance to drinking water systems to address perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.

Requires a drinking water management plan for private or public schools. Plans must include water sampling and testing
at each filtered bottle-filling station and filtered faucet in the school for lead, PFOS, and PFOA.
Prohibits intentionally added PFAS in cosmetics, carpet and fabric treatments, food packaging, and cookware. Requires manufacturers to label certain products containing PFAS.
Authorizes bonds to fund remediation of wells contaminated by PFAS in Franklin.

Prohibits intentionally added PFAS in certain products including food packaging, cleaning products, cosmetics, and textile furnishings. Allows the Department of Natural Resources to require manufacturers to test certain products for PFAS.
Appropriations bill. Includes $12 million to Village of Ridgewood for drinking water infrastructure improvements related PFAS pollution.
Requires public water systems and landlords to provide certain notice of elevated PFAS levels in drinking water.
Requires the Department of Environmental Protection to study feasibility of using alternative water supply source when PFAS exceed maximum contaminant level.
Requires certain water purveyors to identify, and use, alternative water supply source when PFAS exceed maximum contaminant levels.
Restricts bisphenols in juvenile products, effective January 1, 2026.

Requires producers of firefighting personal protective equipment that contains intentionally added PFAS to provide written notice to purchasers. Prohibits the the sale, manufacture, and distribution of firefighting personal protective equipment that contains intentionally added PFAS.

Requires the Department of Natural Resources to create a municipal PFAS grant program, providing financial assistance for PFAS testing and remediation efforts across municipal water systems, nonmunicipal entities, and privately owned landfills.
Prohibits sale, manufacture, and distribution of certain apparel containing intentionally added PFAS.
Authorizes the California State Water Resources Control Board to fund projects that address PFAS in drinking water and source water.
Requires PFAS monitoring and reporting for every industrial wastewater source that discharges pollutants.
Allocates $1,500,000 to the Department of Emergency Services and Public Protection to support the removal of PFAS from fire apparatus.
Appropriations bill. Provides $5 million for PFAS mitigation.
Establishes a unit to study PFAS exposure and cancer rates among Illinois firefighters.

Requires PFAS testing and reporting for discharge wastewater. Requires annual testing for PFAS in sludge and sludge-derived compost, and it prohibits the application or sale of sludge from wastewater treatment plants for agricultural purposes.
Establishes a working group within the Department of Health in Hawaii to investigate the environmental dangers posed by PFAS and related chemicals.
Requires testing for PFAS in sewage sludge and other residual materials intended for land application.
Requires testing of PFAS for biosolids.

Issue/Sector: Artificial Turf, Firefighting Foam, Children's Products, Personal Care Products, Textiles
Description: Prohibits intentionally added PFAS in certain products including artificial turf, carpets or rugs, cookware, cosmetics, fabric treatments, juvenile products, menstrual products, ski wax, and textile articles. Prohibits the manufacture, sale, or use of firefighting foam containing PFAS. Requires disclosure of PFAS in firefighting personal protective equipment.
Requires annual testing of drinking water from all public water systems for PFAS.
Prohibits the sale or distribution of menstrual products containing PFAS.

Issue/Sector: Personal Care Products, Artificial Turf, Textiles
Description: Prohibits the sale or distribution of certain outdoor apparel intended for extreme or extended use in severe wet conditions that contain intentionally added PFAS chemicals. Prohibits PFAS in cleaning products, cookware, dental floss, menstruation products, ski wax, and textile articles. Prohibits a person from installing artificial turf that contains intentionally added PFAS chemicals on state property.

Requires the owner or operator of a publicly owned treatment works to monitor PFAS levels in effluent, influent, and biosolids at least quarterly and report all such monitoring data. Requires the Department of Environmental Quality to develop a PFAS action plan to identify and address sources of certain PFAS. Requires certain facilities that manufacture or use PFAS to report the use of such chemicals to the Department and to monitor such PFAS.
Prohibits the manufacture and sale of surfboard wax containing PFAS.

Issue/Sector: Cleaning Products, Textiles, Personal Care Products, Children's Products
Description: Prohibits the sale or distribution of the following products containing intentionally added PFAS: apparel, carpets or rugs, cleaning products, cookware, cosmetics, dental floss, fabric treatments, children’s products, menstruation products, textile furnishings, ski wax, and upholstered furniture. Requires manufacturers to submit information on products containing intentionally added PFAS to the state.
Requires a plan to phase out PFAS chemicals in firefighting materials at Richmond International Airport by 2028.
Prohibits the purchase, installation, or use of artificial turf by a municipality, school district, or State department or agency for a new or existing field.
Establishes a pilot program aimed at funding research related to PFAS.
Establishes a grant program aimed at enabling research institutions to conduct a pilot program that assesses the levels and effects of PFAS in the blood of qualified participants.
Establishes a moratorium on land application of sludge and biosolids until July 1, 2028.
Allocates funding for various environmental and natural resource projects, including grants for water infrastructure addressing PFAS contamination.
Requires disclosure of PFAS in pesticides.
Requires monitoring and reporting on microplastics and PFAS in Lake Michigan and the Illinois drinking water system.
Establishes maximum contaminant levels for contaminants such as PFAS, PFOA, PFOS, hexavalent chromium, and 1,4-Dioxane.
Requires monthly testing for PFAS in sewage sludge starting January 1, 2027, with strict limits on application.
Extending statute of limitations for hazardous substances.
Requires testing of biosolids for PFAS.
Requires testing and monitoring of wastewater discharges including biosolids for PFAS substances.

Appropriations bill. Allocates for regional drinking water infrastructure as part of Phase 2 of the southern New Hampshire regional water project to increase water supply by over 2 million gallons per day to multiple towns impacted by per- and polyfluoroalkyl substance contamination and growing water demands.
Extending statute of limitations for hazardous substances.
Establishes a program for managing biosolids, focusing on PFAS chemical testing and analysis.
Appropriations bill. Includes funding for PFAS cleanup and to help identify additional priority consumer products containing PFAS for potential regulatory action.
Appropriations bill. Includes funding to the Department of Ecology for PFAS response and cleanup.
Requires disclosure of PFAS usage by industrial users to publicly owned treatment works.

Prohibits food vendor from using polystyrene foam container in sales of prepared food. Prohibits person from selling or distributing foodware containers containing perfluoroalkyl or polyfluoroalkyl substances.

Toxic Chemical: Formaldehyde, Phthalates, Triclosan, Toxic Chemicals General, PFAS, Mercury
Description: Requires Oregon Health Authority to adopt and maintain list of designated high priority chemicals of concern used in cosmetic products and to periodically review and revise list. Requires manufacturers of cosmetic products sold in state to include on manufacturer’s website notice of certain chemicals used in products, beginning on January 1, 2027. Bans manufacture, sale and distribution of cosmetic products containing certain chemicals and classes of chemicals in state, beginning on January 1, 2027.
Requires notification for counties and tribes of an exceedance of groundwater protection standards or standards for PFAS.
Prohibits certain intentionally added ingredients in cosmetics including phthalates, formaldehyde, mercury, parabens, certain dyes, and various PFAS.

Establishes reporting requirements for oil and gas operators, who must confirm that their chemical products do not contain intentionally added PFAS chemicals and provide detailed information about the chemicals used in hydraulic fracturing.

Safer Solution: Setting The Stage, Phasing Out the Bad
Description: Allocates funds for PFAS mitigation and research, establishes drinking water standards, and sets discharge limits for industrial users to address PFAS contamination.
Prohibits the use of a firefighting substance that contains a perfluoroalkyl substance or polyfluoroalkyl substance. Establishes a firefighting substances disposal reimbursement program.

Toxic Chemical: Bisphenols, Cadmium, Lead, PFAS, Mercury, Phthalates, Toxic Chemicals General
Safer Solution: Promoting Safer Solutions, Phasing Out the Bad
Description: Requires producers of plastic packaging and certain other plastic products to reduce quantity of plastic sold. Prohibits the sale of packaging or products containing specific toxic substances, including lead, cadmium, mercury, hexavalent chromium, certain PFAS, ortho-phthalates, and bisphenols.

Toxic Chemical: Heavy Metals, Toxic Chemicals General, Phthalates, PFAS, Formaldehyde, Bisphenols
Safer Solution: Safer Management Systems, Phasing Out the Bad
Description: Packaging reduction bill that includes a prohibition on the sale of packaging that contains certain substances, including toxic metals, PFAS, and vinyl chloride.

Issue/Sector: Children's Products, Cleaning Products, Personal Care Products, Packaging, Textiles
Description: Prohibits the manufacture, sale, or distribution of certain products containing intentionally added PFAS, including, cleaning products, cookware, cosmetics and personal care products, feminine hygiene products, pet food package, juvenile products, intimacy products, fabric treatments, ski wax, textiles, furnishings, and paint. Requires state department to identify currently unavoidable uses of PFAS.
Appropriations bill. Transfers $110 million from the general fund to the PFAS fund. Transfers $15 million from the environmental management fund to the PFAS fund.
Prohibits land application of sludge containing a certain level of PFAS.

Requires the Division of Public Health to create a website where Delaware residents can find out the level of PFAS in their public drinking water systems. Requires the Division to notify public water utilities if the PFAS in their water exceeds certain limits.

Toxic Chemical: Toxic Chemicals General, Phthalates, PFAS, Bisphenols, Toxic Flame Retardants, Formaldehyde
Issue/Sector: Packaging, Packaging Reduction, Polystyrene, PVC, Plastics
Safer Solution: Phasing Out the Bad, Safer Management Systems
Description: Establishes a comprehensive packaging reduction and recycling program. Requires producers to reduce packaging. Prohibits the use of certain toxic substances in packaging including phthalates, bisphenols, PFAS, halogenated flame retardants, formaldehyde, and more.
Prohibits the sale of medical adhesives and bandages containing PFAS.
Allocates $8.2 million for PFAS management plans in municipalities.

Authorizes the New York state environmental facilities corporation to award grants and loans to community water systems and non-community water systems for water quality projects that relate to the removal of PFAS.
Prohibits the sale and manufacture of photovoltaic modules containing PFAS.

Prohibits the sale of certain products that contain PFAS, including air care products, automotive products, cleaning products, polish or floor maintenance products, cookware, fabric treatment, rugs, ski wax, textiles and textile articles. Requires manufacturers of products containing PFAS to provide notice.
Prohibits the sale, use, and disposal of firefighting foam containing PFAS. Establishes a program to ensure the safe collection and disposal of PFAS-containing firefighting foam.
Prohibits the sale of anti-fogging sprays containing PFAS.
Requires coverage for PFAS blood testing in health insurance policies and social services.

Restricts the manufacture, sale, distribution, and use of class B firefighting foam containing intentionally added PFAS. Requires warning labels on firefighting protective equipment that contain PFAS.

Imposes a 50% gross revenues tax on PFAS product manufacturers and retailers in Minnesota, establishes a cleanup fund for environmental purposes, and creates an advisory commission to oversee fund allocation.
Requires testing of PFAS in biosolids.
Requires the state to establish a master contract for firefighting foam that excludes products with PFAS.
Requires labeling of firefighter equipment containing PFAS chemicals.
Allocates $40 million for improvements to Apple Valley’s water treatment plant specifically to address PFAS in drinking water
Allocates $1.6 million for designing water treatment improvements targeting PFAS contamination in Oak Park Heights’ drinking water.
Allocates $1,075,000 for PFAS biomonitoring in eastern communities.
Allocates $5.5 million for a demonstration project to treat landfill leachate for PFAS and other contaminants.
Allocates funds for a demonstration project to treat landfill leachate for PFAS and other contaminants.
Requires testing of PFAS in biosolids. Imposes a five-year moratorium on the spreading of biosolids.
Creates liability and penalties for contaminating water supplies with perfluorinated chemicals.
Appropriations bill. Provides $12.75 million to the North Carolina Collaboratory to conduct PFAS analysis.

Prohibits PFAS and high priority chemicals of concern in children’s products. Requires the Department of Environmental Protection to maintain and update a publicly accessible list of toxic chemicals of concern.

Establishes the Agricultural PFAS Relief Fund to assist farmers with costs related to PFAS contamination. Establishes the Agricultural Fertilizer Purchasing Fund to supports farmers facing increased fertilizer costs due to PFAS-related restrictions.
Requires testing and monitoring of PFAS in biosolids.
Appropriations for fiscal year 2021-2022. Provides a one-time appropriation of $14,450,000 for PFAS remediation. Provides $20,276,000 for PFAS and environmental contamination response.
Prohibits the manufacture, sale, distribution, and use of firefighting foam containing intentionally added PFAS. Effective January 1, 2025.
Appropriates funding for PFAS remediation projects.

Prohibits the manufacture and sale of firefighting foam containing PFAS, prohibits the use of PFAS foam for training purposes, requires manufacturers of firefighter protective equipment to disclose the inclusion of PFAS in their products. Effective January 1, 2022.
Prohibits the use of PFAS-containing firefighting foam for training purposes or testing purposes.
Allows state Water Board to require monitoring for and reporting of PFAS by water utilities.
Prohibits the use of PFAS-containing firefighting foam for training purposes or testing purposes.
Appropriations bill. Includes allocating $50,000,000 for technical and financial assistance to drinking water systems to address perfluoroalkyl and polyfluoroalkyl substances.
Appropriations bill. Provides $30,000,000 for technical and financial assistance to drinking water systems to address PFAS.

Creates fund PFAS cash fund, used to fund the PFAS grant program, the PFAS takeback program, and provide technical assistance in locating and studying PFAS to communities, stakeholders, and relevant regulatory bodies.
Provides $1,000,000 for the University of Florida PFAS Contaminated Material Treatment Pilot project.
Prohibits the manufacture, sale, use, and purchase of firefighting foams containing PFAS.
Requires the commissioner of the department of environmental services to adopt a state drinking water standard, and ambient and surface groundwater standards for perflurochemicals.
Prohibits the use of PFAS-containing firefighting foam for training purposes.

Requires cosmetics manufacturers to disclose lists of chemicals, flavors, and fragrances included in their products to the state along with associated health hazards. Requires the state to maintain a public website displaying the information.

Requires the recyclability of a product or packaging to be truthful. Manufacturers would no longer be allowed to put the “chasing arrows” recycling symbol on items that aren’t actually recyclable. Products that contain PFAS chemicals can’t be labeled as recyclable.

Toxic Chemical: Bisphenols, PFAS, Toxic Flame Retardants, Toxic Chemicals General, Phthalates, Heavy Metals
Description: The Oregon Toxic Free Kids Act (TFKA) establishes a list of high priority chemicals of concern for children’s products, posts the list online, requires manufacturer disclosure of these chemicals, and requires their eventual removal. Authorizes participation in the Interstate Chemicals Clearinghouse. Please note: The Oregon Health Authority (OHA) is in its final stages of implementing TFKA. The current final phase of rulemaking focuses on the exemptions manufacturers may seek if they are unable to completely phase out these toxic chemicals. OHA must finalize these rules in order for TFKA to be fully enforceable. Once that happens, OEC will continue watchdogging the law to ensure that manufacturers are abiding by its requirements.

Reforms aspects of California’s Safer Consumer Products program. Makes California’s program more efficient by granting data call-in authority to the program, streamlining processes (including giving the program the authority to regulate on the basis of alternatives analyses published by other governments or in the peer-reviewed literature), and setting clear timelines and more transparent work plans.

Toxic Chemical: Phthalates, Bisphenols, Toxic Chemicals General, Toxic Flame Retardants, PFAS
Description: Directs the Department of Ecology to identify and take regulatory action on consumer products that are a significant source of chemicals that are a concern for sensitive populations and species. Prioritizes PCBs, PFAS, organohalogen flame retardants, phthalates, and phenolic compounds (BPA, APEs) for initial consideration.

Prohibits the manufacture and sale of class B firefighting foam containing PFAS chemicals. Requires manufacturers of firefighting personal protective equipment (PPE) to notify buyers in Washington if their products contain PFAS.
Requires DEQ to test wastewater treatment plants for persistent, bioaccumulative, and toxic chemicals, including PFAS.

Prohibits the use of firefighting foam containing PFAS and establishes a take-back program for such products. Effective October 1, 2021. The bill also bans intentionally added PFAS in food packaging no later than December 31, 2023.

Safer Solution: Accountability, Phasing Out the Bad, Setting The Stage
Description: An omnibus bill that provides $600,000 in funding to develop and implement an initiative to reduce sources of PFAS upstream of wastewater treatment facilities; provides approximately $1.4 million in funding to develop strategies to manage PFAS in land-applied biosolids; provides $250,000 in funding to demonstrate new technology for protecting drinking water from PFAS; and prohibits the manufacture, sale, and distribution of food packaging containing intentionally added PFAS with an implementation date of Jan 1, 2024.
Appropriations bill. Includes funding for implementation of PFAS regulation, PFAS reduction grants, and more.
